event: Paleo-Indian peoples, ancestors of Native Americans, are well established across North America after migrating from Asia.
Clovis culture, known for distinctive fluted stone spear points, spreads widely across the continent.
The Adena culture builds elaborate burial mounds in the Ohio River Valley.
The Hopewell tradition develops extensive trade networks and monumental earthworks across eastern North America.
The Mississippian culture rises, building large mound centers and maize-based chiefdoms.
Cahokia near present-day St. Louis becomes the largest pre-Columbian city north of Mexico.
Ancestral Puebloans construct cliff dwellings and great houses at sites such as Mesa Verde and Chaco Canyon.
Christopher Columbus reaches the Caribbean, initiating sustained European contact with the Americas.
Spanish settlers found St. Augustine in Florida, the oldest continuously inhabited European settlement in the present-day U.S.
English colonists establish Jamestown in Virginia, the first permanent English settlement in North America.
The first enslaved Africans are brought to Virginia, beginning slavery in English North America.
The Pilgrims arrive on the Mayflower and found Plymouth Colony in present-day Massachusetts.
The French and Indian War begins, part of the global Seven Years' War contesting control of North America.
The Stamp Act provokes colonial protest against taxation without representation.
Colonists stage the Boston Tea Party in protest against British tea taxes.
The American Revolutionary War erupts with battles at Lexington and Concord.
The Continental Congress adopts the Declaration of Independence on July 4.
The Treaty of Paris ends the Revolutionary War and Britain recognizes American independence.
The Constitutional Convention drafts the United States Constitution in Philadelphia.
George Washington is inaugurated as the first President under the new federal government.
The Bill of Rights, the first ten amendments to the Constitution, is ratified.
The Louisiana Purchase from France roughly doubles the size of the young nation.
The War of 1812 begins against Britain over trade, impressment, and territorial disputes.
The Indian Removal Act forces the displacement of Native nations, leading to the Trail of Tears.
The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo ends the Mexican-American War and cedes vast western territory to the U.S.
The Civil War begins as Southern states secede over slavery and states' rights.
President Lincoln issues the Emancipation Proclamation declaring enslaved people in rebel states free.
The Civil War ends, Lincoln is assassinated, and the Thirteenth Amendment abolishes slavery.
The first transcontinental railroad is completed, linking the eastern and western United States.
The massacre at Wounded Knee marks the violent close of the Indian Wars and the western frontier.
The Spanish-American War makes the U.S. a colonial power, acquiring the Philippines, Puerto Rico, and Guam.
The United States enters World War I on the side of the Allies.
The Nineteenth Amendment grants women the right to vote nationwide.
The Wall Street Crash triggers the Great Depression.
President Franklin Roosevelt launches the New Deal to combat the Depression.
Japan attacks Pearl Harbor, bringing the United States into World War II.
The U.S. drops atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki to end World War II and becomes a founding member of the UN.
The United States leads UN forces in the Korean War against communist advances.
The Montgomery Bus Boycott energizes the modern civil rights movement.
President John F. Kennedy is assassinated in Dallas, Texas.
The Civil Rights Act outlaws discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin.
Apollo 11 lands the first humans on the Moon.
The U.S. withdraws combat troops from Vietnam after a long and divisive war.
President Richard Nixon resigns over the Watergate scandal.
The Cold War ends with the dissolution of the Soviet Union, leaving the U.S. as the sole superpower.
The September 11 terrorist attacks kill nearly 3,000 people and launch the global War on Terror.
A financial crisis originating in U.S. markets triggers the Great Recession.
Barack Obama is inaugurated as the first African American president.
The COVID-19 pandemic causes mass disruption and over a million eventual U.S. deaths.
Rioters storm the U.S. Capitol on January 6 in an attempt to overturn the 2020 election results.
